symptoms: Chronic dyspepsia, weight loss, or jaundice are warning signs for significant GI disease and warrant further investigation. Patients may also report gradual onset or episodic flares depending on the disease course.

symptoms: Polyuria, polydipsia, unexplained weight change and fatigue commonly indicate metabolic/endocrine dysfunction. Temperature intolerance or hair changes may point to thyroid disease. Patients may also report gradual onset or episodic flares depending on the disease course.
tests: Hormone panels specific to suspected disorder (e.g., TSH/FT4 for thyroid disease), fasting glucose and HbA1c for diabetes. Dynamic testing for adrenal/pituitary disorders when indicated. Further targeted testing is guided by clinical findings and specialist consultation.

symptoms: Reduced urine output, edema, and fatigue; flank pain may suggest obstructive causes. Hematuria or foamy urine suggests glomerular pathology. Patients may also report gradual onset or episodic flares depending on the disease course.
tests: Serum creatinine and eGFR to assess kidney function, urinalysis and urine microscopy. Renal imaging (ultrasound or CT) and serologic tests for glomerulonephritis if indicated. Further targeted testing is guided by clinical findings and specialist consultation.

symptoms: Joint pain, stiffness (often worse in the morning for inflammatory causes), and reduced range of motion. Mechanical symptoms such as locking or instability suggest structural pathology. Patients may also report gradual onset or episodic flares depending on the disease course.
tests: Plain radiographs (X-ray) for bony injury, MRI for soft tissue and ligament pathology. Joint aspiration with synovial fluid analysis when infection or crystal arthropathy is suspected. Further targeted testing is guided by clinical findings and specialist consultation.

symptoms: Dysuria, frequency, urgency and suprapubic discomfort suggest lower urinary tract involvement. Hematuria or flank pain suggests upper tract or serious urologic pathology. Patients may also report gradual onset or episodic flares depending on the disease course.
